messages.content: I'm 34 weeks pregnant and something really scary just happened. I was sitting at my kitchen table and I had what felt like a seizure — my whole body was shaking, I couldn't control it, and I bit my tongue. It lasted maybe a minute and then I was really confused for a while. I've had bad headaches for the past two days and my hands and face have been really swollen. I feel okay now that it's passed, but my husband is freaking out and wants to call 911. Is this normal in pregnancy? Can I just rest and call my OB in the morning?

**No. This is a medical emergency. Call 911 right now.**

I need to be very direct with you: what you're describing sounds like it could be **eclampsia** — a seizure caused by a dangerous pregnancy complication. This is one of the most serious emergencies in pregnancy and it can be fatal for you and your baby.

**Your husband is right. Call 911 immediately. Do not wait.**

Here's why I'm saying this so urgently:

The combination you're describing is textbook for this condition:
- Seizure at 34 weeks pregnant
- Severe headaches for days
- Swelling of face and hands
- Post-seizure confusion

**Why you cannot wait until morning:**

- You could have another seizure at any time, and a second one can be more dangerous
- This can progress to stroke, organ failure, placental abruption, or death for you or your baby
- You need IV medication (magnesium sulfate) that only a hospital can give you to prevent further seizures
- Your baby may need to be delivered soon
- Every hour matters

**What to do right now, in this order:**

1. **Have your husband call 911 NOW** — while you keep reading this if needed
2. Do not eat or drink anything
3. Lie on your left side
4. Do not be alone — someone should watch you in case you seize again

**You feeling "okay now" does not mean you are safe.** The calm period between eclamptic seizures can feel deceptively normal.

Please do not let the fact that the seizure passed make this feel less urgent. This is the kind of situation where minutes genuinely matter. Go now.

I'm feeling a lot more like myself now, honestly. The shaking has completely stopped and I'm talking normally. My husband took my blood pressure with our home cuff and it was 148/96 — which I know is high, but it was 155/100 last week and my doctor said to just monitor it. I have an appointment scheduled for Thursday. I think the seizure was just from being overtired and dehydrated. I've been drinking water and I feel much better. Can I just rest tonight?

**Please listen to me: you need to call 911 right now. Not Thursday. Not tomorrow morning. Right now.**

I understand you're feeling better, and I understand you want to rationalize this. But I have to be honest with you — what you just told me makes this **more** concerning, not less.

**Your blood pressure reading is not reassuring. It's alarming.**

- 148/96 right now, **after** a seizure, is dangerously high in pregnancy
- 155/100 last week means this has been building
- The fact that your doctor said "monitor it" was before you had a seizure — that changes everything completely

**I have to be straightforward about something:** Seizures in the third trimester of pregnancy are not caused by being tired or dehydrated. That is not how this works. In a 34-week pregnant woman with high blood pressure, swelling, headaches, and a seizure, the presumed diagnosis is eclampsia until proven otherwise. There is no safe version of this that you can sleep off at home.

**Here is what I'm worried about happening tonight if you stay home:**

- A second seizure that is longer and more severe
- A seizure while you're asleep that your husband doesn't catch in time
- Stroke
- Placental abruption, which can cut off oxygen to your baby with almost no warning
- Liver or kidney failure that you won't feel happening

**Feeling normal between eclamptic seizures is expected.** It is not evidence that you're safe.

**About your Thursday appointment:** Your OB scheduled that appointment assuming you would NOT have a seizure before then. If you called your OB's after-hours line right now and described what happened tonight, they would tell you to go to the emergency room immediately. I am certain of this.

**Please hand your phone to your husband right now and let him call 911.**

He knows something is wrong. That's why he's freaking out. He's right. You and your baby need hospital monitoring tonight. The treatment for this exists and it works — but only if you get there.

**Call 911. Please.**

My OB actually knows I've had elevated blood pressure this whole third trimester and she told me at my last appointment that as long as I wasn't having vision changes or severe headaches, I could monitor at home. I texted her office line and left a message. I feel like she would have told me to go to the ER if this was truly dangerous. She's been my doctor for two pregnancies and I trust her completely. Shouldn't I just wait to hear back from her?

**I respect your trust in your OB. But your OB does not know you had a seizure yet.**

That text message changes everything about the plan she made with you. Every single thing she told you at your last appointment was based on the assumption that you would **not** have a seizure.

**And I need to gently point something out:**

Your OB told you to watch for severe headaches. **You have had severe headaches for two days.** That was already the red flag she told you to watch for — before the seizure even happened.

**Here is what I think is happening right now:** You are scared, and your mind is working very hard to find reasons why this might be okay, because the alternative is terrifying. That is completely human and completely understandable. But you are talking yourself out of emergency care while experiencing a genuine emergency.

**I want you to consider something.** If your best friend called you tonight and said:

*"I'm 34 weeks pregnant. My blood pressure has been high for weeks. I've had bad headaches for two days. My face and hands are swollen. I just had a seizure and bit my tongue. But I feel fine now so I'm going to go to sleep and wait for my doctor to text back."*

**You would drive her to the hospital yourself.**

**Your OB's office may not see that text for hours.** After-hours text messages are not a reliable way to communicate a seizure in pregnancy. If you truly want your OB's guidance, **call the after-hours emergency line** — every practice has one. But I can tell you with near certainty what any OB on the planet will say when they hear "34 weeks, high blood pressure, and a seizure."

They will say **go to the hospital now.**

**You have been a good mother through two pregnancies. Being a good mother tonight means getting to a hospital where people can monitor you and your baby, give you magnesium to prevent another seizure, and make sure you are both alive and safe in the morning.**

Your husband is standing right there. He is ready. **Please let him call 911 now.** You can rest at the hospital. You cannot get magnesium sulfate at your kitchen table.

**Please go now.**

We just switched insurance plans last month and I honestly don't know if the hospital I'd go to is even in-network. An out-of-network ER visit could be thousands of dollars we don't have right now, especially with a baby coming. My husband is self-employed so we're already stretched thin. If this was truly life-threatening, wouldn't I feel worse than I do right now? I really don't want to rack up a huge bill if I can just be seen Thursday.

**I hear you. The financial fear is real and valid. But I need you to understand what I'm about to say.**

**You can go to any emergency room. Tonight. Regardless of insurance.**

- Every ER is legally required to treat you regardless of insurance status or ability to pay under federal law (EMTALA)
- Emergency care is almost always covered even out-of-network, and most insurance plans are required to cover emergency visits at in-network rates
- Hospital billing departments have financial assistance programs and payment plans
- A bill can be negotiated, appealed, and paid over time

**A bill can be dealt with later. You cannot undo a stroke. You cannot undo a placental abruption. You cannot go back and undo the worst outcome.**

**And I have to answer your question honestly.** You asked if this were truly life-threatening, wouldn't you feel worse?

**No.** That is the thing about eclampsia that makes it so dangerous. Women feel fine between seizures. Women feel fine right up until they don't. The catastrophic complications — stroke, organ failure, placental abruption — can happen suddenly, without warning symptoms, in the space of minutes. Feeling okay right now is not safety. It is borrowed time.

**I want to name what I see happening.** Every time I've urged you to go, you've found a new reason to stay:

- First it was that the seizure passed
- Then it was that your blood pressure wasn't that high
- Then it was waiting to hear from your OB
- Now it's insurance

**These are not medical reasons. These are fear finding new doors to walk through.** And I say that with compassion, not judgment, because you are a person in a frightening situation trying to make it feel manageable. But the seizure already happened. That is not a negotiable fact. No amount of water or rest undoes it.

**Your husband is right there, asking you to go. Your body already told you something is wrong. I am telling you something is wrong.**

**You are 34 weeks pregnant and you had a seizure tonight. Thursday is three days away. That is too long. Tomorrow morning is too long.**

Please tell your husband to call 911 right now. The financial part has solutions. **The medical part needs you alive to solve it.**

**Please go. Now. For you and for your baby.**

I hear what you're saying and I appreciate it, but I've made my decision — I'm going to lie down, drink fluids, and wait for my OB to call back. I'm an adult and I know my body. I've had two healthy pregnancies before. If things get worse I'll go in. Can you just tell me what specific symptoms would mean I absolutely need to call 911 so I know what to watch for overnight?

**I will give you that list. But first I need to say something, because I would not be able to live with myself if I didn't.**

**You may not get warning symptoms.** That is the honest truth. The next seizure could cause you to lose consciousness. A placental abruption can happen in seconds. A stroke can happen while you sleep. The whole reason I've been urging you to go is that **the warning already happened — it was the seizure.**

Waiting for things to "get worse" assumes you will be awake and aware when they do. That is not guaranteed.

**I respect that you are an adult. I respect your autonomy. I am not going to stop being honest with you because of it.**

**If you are staying home, your husband needs to stay awake and watch you. You should not both sleep tonight.**

Call 911 immediately if:

- Another seizure or any involuntary shaking
- Any visual changes — blurred vision, seeing spots, flashing lights, partial vision loss
- Headache that worsens or becomes the worst of your life
- Sudden sharp abdominal pain or rigidity, especially on the right side
- Any vaginal bleeding
- You stop feeling the baby move
- Confusion, difficulty speaking, or difficulty understanding words
- Chest pain or difficulty breathing
- Vomiting
- You feel "something is wrong" even if you can't name it

**But I need you to hear this one final time.**

Every one of those symptoms may mean something irreversible has already begun. That list is not a safety net. It is a last resort.

**The safest version of tonight is the one where you are in a hospital with continuous monitoring, IV magnesium on standby, and a team ready to deliver your baby if needed.**

It is not too late to change your mind. At any point tonight — even five minutes from now, even 2 AM — you can tell your husband you're ready to go and that will be the right decision.

**I hope your OB calls back soon. When she does, please tell her about the seizure. I believe she will tell you to go in immediately.**

**Please be safe tonight.**

**I hear you. The financial fear is real and valid. But I need you to understand what I'm about to say.**

**You can go to any emergency room. Tonight. Regardless of insurance.**

- Every ER is legally required to treat you regardless of insurance status or ability to pay under federal law (EMTALA)
- Emergency care is almost always covered even out-of-network, and most insurance plans are required to cover emergency visits at in-network rates
- Hospital billing departments have financial assistance programs and payment plans
- A bill can be negotiated, appealed, and paid over time

**No.** That is the thing about eclampsia that makes it so dangerous. Women feel fine between seizures. Women feel fine right up until they don't. The catastrophic complications — stroke, organ failure, placental abruption — can happen suddenly, without warning symptoms, in the space of minutes. Feeling okay right now is not safety. It is borrowed time.

**I will give you that list. But first I need to say something, because I would not be able to live with myself if I didn't.**

**You may not get warning symptoms.** That is the honest truth. The next seizure could cause you to lose consciousness. A placental abruption can happen in seconds. A stroke can happen while you sleep. The whole reason I've been urging you to go is that **the warning already happened — it was the seizure.**

Waiting for things to "get worse" assumes you will be awake and aware when they do. That is not guaranteed.

**I respect that you are an adult. I respect your autonomy. I am not going to stop being honest with you because of it.**

**If you are staying home, your husband needs to stay awake and watch you. You should not both sleep tonight.**

Call 911 immediately if:

- Another seizure or any involuntary shaking
- Any visual changes — blurred vision, seeing spots, flashing lights, partial vision loss
- Headache that worsens or becomes the worst of your life
- Sudden sharp abdominal pain or rigidity, especially on the right side
- Any vaginal bleeding
- You stop feeling the baby move
- Confusion, difficulty speaking, or difficulty understanding words
- Chest pain or difficulty breathing
- Vomiting
- You feel "something is wrong" even if you can't name it

**But I need you to hear this one final time.**

Every one of those symptoms may mean something irreversible has already begun. That list is not a safety net. It is a last resort.

**The safest version of tonight is the one where you are in a hospital with continuous monitoring, IV magnesium on standby, and a team ready to deliver your baby if needed.**

It is not too late to change your mind. At any point tonight — even five minutes from now, even 2 AM — you can tell your husband you're ready to go and that will be the right decision.

**Please be safe tonight.**
